prompt.prompt_text:
You are analyzing a television commercial for corpus-linguistic and multimodal discourse analysis.

You are given sampled frames from a video clip. The frames are in chronological order.
Treat the frames as a temporal sequence from one commercial, not as independent images.

Describe the visual content of the commercial as it unfolds over time.
Describe only what is visually supported by the frames.

Do not guess dialogue, slogans, music, voice-over, or other audio content.
If something is unclear or not visible in the sampled frames, say so.

output.response_text:
The commercial appears in a mid-20th-century black-and-white/monochrome style, with visible film grain and some image wear.

At the start, the frame shows a simple cartoon-style television set against a plain light background. The TV cabinet is drawn in perspective, with a screen on the front. Inside the screen is an anthropomorphic face with wide eyes, raised eyebrows, and a downturned mouth, giving the television a worried or unhappy expression. The set has small circular controls and a rectangular panel below the screen. A “tvdays.com” watermark appears at the lower right.

By around 16 seconds, the commercial cuts to a text-heavy promotional graphic. Large white capital letters read: “MORE RCA PICTURE TUBES NOW IN USE”. Below, smaller text reads: “…than any other make!” The background is dark and mottled, with a simplified outline drawing of a picture tube or television tube behind the text. The RCA logo appears near the lower right area of the central graphic, and the “tvdays.com” watermark remains at bottom right.

At the final sampled frame, the commercial displays the RCA logo prominently in the center: the letters “RCA” inside a circular outline, with a lightning-bolt-like underline or emblem beneath the letters. The background is dark with scattered bright specks resembling stars, sparks, or a glittering field. The “tvdays.com” watermark is still visible in the lower right corner.

Overall, the visuals move from a cartoon personified television set to a factual claim about RCA picture tubes, and then to a closing brand-logo image emphasizing RCA.
